title.emphasis: Appendix: Synoptic Chart of 100 Landmark First Amendment Cases

row.entry: Upheld conviction for anti-draft leaflets, establishing that speech can be restricted if it poses a clear and present danger.
Held that the First Amendment's protections of speech and press apply to the states through the Fourteenth Amendment.
Upheld a conviction for syndicalism, but is famous for Brandeis's concurrence arguing speech can only be punished if danger is imminent.
Struck down a state law banning red flags, recognizing symbolic acts as a form of protected speech.
Established a strong presumption against prior restraint, striking down a law that allowed enjoining "malicious" newspapers.
Incorporated the freedom of assembly, holding that peaceful assembly for lawful discussion cannot be made a crime.
Invalidated a permit ordinance, establishing streets and parks as traditional public forums for speech and assembly.
Incorporated the Free Exercise Clause and struck down a licensing scheme for religious solicitation.
Upheld a permit system for parades, allowing reasonable, content-neutral "time, place, and manner" restrictions.
Upheld a conviction for insults, defining unprotected "fighting words" as those that inflict injury or incite a breach of the peace.
*West Virginia State Board of Ed. v. Barnette*, 319 U.S. 624
Ruled that compelling public school students to salute the flag violates the First Amendment.
Incorporated the Establishment Clause and invoked the "wall of separation," though it upheld transportation reimbursements for parochial school students.
Found unconstitutional a "released time" program for religious instruction held within public school buildings.
Overturned a breach-of-the-peace conviction, holding that speech is protected even if it "stirs people to anger."
Upheld Smith Act convictions of communists, using a balancing test that weakened the "clear and present danger" standard.
Upheld a "released time" program where students left school grounds for religious instruction.
Narrowed the Smith Act, distinguishing between protected advocacy of abstract doctrine and unprotected advocacy of illegal action.
*NAACP v. Alabama ex rel. Patterson*, 357 U.S. 449
Protected the NAACP from having to disclose its membership list, recognizing a constitutional right to freedom of association.
Struck down a state requirement that public officeholders declare a belief in God.
Ruled that state-sponsored, non-denominational prayer in public schools is unconstitutional.
Protected peaceful civil rights marchers, holding states cannot criminalize the peaceful expression of unpopular views.
Protected the NAACP's right to sponsor civil rights litigation as a form of protected political expression and association.
Held that denying unemployment benefits to someone who refused to work on her Sabbath burdened her religion and required a compelling state interest.
Struck down state-mandated Bible readings in public schools and established the "secular purpose and primary effect" test.
*New York Times Co. v. Sullivan*, 376 U.S. 254
Established that public officials must prove "actual malice" to win a defamation suit.
Upheld a conviction for burning a draft card, creating a test for regulating expressive conduct.
Established a balancing test for the free speech rights of public employees.
Affirmed students' free speech rights in public schools, allowing restriction only for "material and substantial disruption."
Established the modern standard that advocacy of illegal action can be punished only if it is directed to and likely to cause imminent lawless action.
Protected offensive speech, ruling the state could not punish a man for wearing a jacket with "Fuck the Draft" in a courthouse.
Created the three-part *Lemon* test for Establishment Clause cases, adding an "excessive entanglement" prong.
*New York Times Co. v. United States*, 403 U.S. 713
Rejected the government's attempt to enjoin publication of the Pentagon Papers, affirming a "heavy presumption" against prior restraint.
Granted Amish parents an exemption from compulsory education laws past the eighth grade.
Held that the First Amendment does not provide reporters with a privilege to refuse to testify before a grand jury.
*California Motor Transport Co. v. Trucking Unlimited*, 404 U.S. 508
Held that the right to petition does not protect "sham" litigation used to harass competitors in violation of antitrust laws.
Established the three-part test for determining whether material is obscene and unprotected.
Held that for private figure plaintiffs, states can set a negligence standard for defamation, but punitive damages require "actual malice."
Created a test to determine if conduct is expressive: intent to convey a message and likelihood it will be understood.
Held that the press cannot be held liable for publishing a rape victim's name obtained from public records.
Upheld contribution limits but struck down expenditure limits in political campaigns, equating spending money with speech.
Ruled that the First Amendment does not prevent police from obtaining a warrant to search a newsroom.
Ruled schools can punish students for lewd and indecent speech that is inconsistent with the school's educational mission.
Held that public figures cannot recover for intentional infliction of emotional distress from a parody without showing a false statement of fact made with actual malice.
Ruled that schools can censor school-sponsored student expression if it is reasonably related to legitimate pedagogical concerns.
Ruled that burning the American flag is a form of expressive conduct protected by the First Amendment.
Held a newspaper could not be liable for publishing a rape victim's name it lawfully obtained from a police report.
Held the Free Exercise Clause does not exempt individuals from neutral, generally applicable laws, even if they burden religious practice.
*R. A. V. v. City of St. Paul*, 505 U.S. 377
Struck down a hate speech ordinance because it selectively banned "fighting words" based on their content (race, religion, etc.).
*Church of the Lukumi Babalu Aye, Inc. v. City of Hialeah*, 508 U.S. 520
Struck down ordinances banning animal sacrifice because they were not neutral but targeted a specific religion.
Afforded speech on the internet the highest level of First Amendment protection, striking down the Communications Decency Act.
*Boy Scouts of America v. Dale*, 530 U.S. 640
Upheld the Boy Scouts' right to exclude a gay scoutmaster, finding forced inclusion would impair the group's expressive message.
Ruled schools can restrict student speech at school events that is reasonably viewed as promoting illegal drug use.
Struck down prohibitions on independent expenditures by corporations and unions in elections.
Protected the right of Westboro Baptist Church to picket a military funeral, shielding even "outrageous" speech on public issues.
Held that the First Amendment prohibits a state from forcing a website designer to create expressive designs speaking messages with which the designer disagrees.
Addressed claims of government coercion of social media platforms, clarifying the standard for when government communication becomes unconstitutional pressure.
Held that social media platforms engage in protected speech and that state laws regulating their content moderation practices implicate the First Amendment.
Held that a government official's coercive threats aimed at chilling a group's advocacy speech could state a plausible First Amendment claim.
*Cochran v. Louisiana State Board of Education*, 281 U.S. 370
Upheld providing secular textbooks to all students, including those in parochial schools, under a "child benefit" theory.
Upheld mandatory flag salutes in schools, a decision that was famously overruled three years later.
Struck down a license tax on soliciting as applied to Jehovah's Witnesses distributing religious literature.
Upheld child labor laws that prevented a Jehovah's Witness from having her young niece distribute religious literature.
Ruled that a privately owned "company town" could not prohibit the distribution of religious literature on its sidewalks.
Held that films are a significant medium for the communication of ideas and are protected by the First Amendment.
Curbed the powers of the House Un-American Activities Committee on First Amendment grounds.
Struck down a California law requiring veterans to sign a loyalty oath to receive a property tax exemption.
Upheld a Sunday closing law ("blue law") against a challenge from Orthodox Jewish merchants.
Struck down a state law requiring public school teachers to sign a loyalty oath.
Struck down an Arkansas law that banned the teaching of evolution in public schools.
Held that a public college could not deny official recognition to a student group simply because of its affiliation with a national organization.
*Virginia State Pharmacy Board v. Virginia Citizens Consumer Council*, 425 U.S. 748
Established that purely commercial speech enjoys First Amendment protection.
Held that New Hampshire could not force a Jehovah's Witness to display the state motto "Live Free or Die" on his license plate.
*First National Bank of Boston v. Bellotti*, 435 U.S. 765
Struck down a state law banning corporate expenditures related to ballot initiatives.
*Central Hudson Gas & Elec. v. Public Service Comm'n*, 447 U.S. 557
Established a four-part test for determining the constitutionality of restrictions on commercial speech.
Struck down a Kentucky law requiring the posting of the Ten Commandments in public school classrooms.
*Heffron v. Int'l Soc. for Krishna Consciousness*, 452 U.S. 640
Upheld a state fair rule limiting solicitation to fixed booths as a valid time, place, and manner restriction.
*Island Trees School District v. Pico*, 457 U.S. 853
Held that school boards cannot remove books from school libraries simply because they dislike the ideas contained in them.
Struck down an Alabama law authorizing a one-minute period of silence for "meditation or voluntary prayer" as lacking a secular purpose.
Struck down a Louisiana law requiring "balanced treatment" for creation science and evolution.
*Westside Community Board of Ed. v. Mergens*, 496 U.S. 226
Upheld the Equal Access Act, which requires public secondary schools to allow student religious groups to meet if other non-curriculum groups can.
Ruled that clergy-led prayer at a public school graduation ceremony is unconstitutional.
*Lamb's Chapel v. Center Moriches Union Free School Dist.*, 508 U.S. 384
Held that a school district could not deny a church access to school premises to show a film series on family values when it allowed other groups to use the premises for social or civic purposes.
Ruled that a public university could not deny funding to a student religious publication while funding other student publications.
Overturned an earlier decision, allowing public school teachers to provide remedial education in parochial schools.
*Santa Fe Independent School Dist. v. Doe*, 530 U.S. 290
Ruled that student-led, student-initiated prayer over the public address system at public school football games is unconstitutional.
*Good News Club v. Milford Central School*, 533 U.S. 98
Held that a school could not exclude a Christian children's club from meeting after hours based on the religious nature of its speech.
Upheld a school voucher program in Cleveland, finding it was neutral toward religion and based on private choice.
*Watchtower Society v. Village of Stratton*, 536 U.S. 150
Struck down a village ordinance requiring a permit for door-to-door advocacy as a violation of free speech rights.
Allowed a Ten Commandments monument on the grounds of the Texas state capitol, finding it had historical as well as religious significance.
Struck down displays of the Ten Commandments in two Kentucky courthouses, finding they had a predominantly religious purpose.
Held that under RFRA, the government had not shown a compelling interest in banning a small religious sect's sacramental use of a hallucinogenic tea.
Ruled that a city's placement of permanent monuments in a public park is a form of government speech and not subject to free speech claims from other groups.
*Hosanna-Tabor Evangelical Lutheran Church & School v. EEOC*, 565 U.S. 171
Recognized a "ministerial exception" to employment discrimination laws, protecting a religious organization's right to choose its ministers.
Upheld a town's practice of opening board meetings with a prayer, even if the prayers were predominantly Christian.
*Burwell v. Hobby Lobby Stores, Inc.*, 573 U.S. 682
Held that under RFRA, closely held for-profit corporations could receive an exemption from a federal mandate on religious grounds.
Struck down a federal law prohibiting the registration of "disparaging" trademarks, finding it to be unconstitutional viewpoint discrimination.
Ruled that a state could not exclude a church from a generally available public benefit program (playground resurfacing) solely because of its religious character.
*American Legion v. American Humanist Association*, 588 U.S. \_\_\_
Allowed a large cross-shaped war memorial to remain on public land, moving away from the *Lemon* test for long-standing monuments.
Struck down a federal law prohibiting the registration of "immoral" or "scandalous" trademarks as unconstitutional viewpoint discrimination.
*Espinoza v. Montana Dept. of Revenue*, 591 U.S. \_\_\_
Ruled that a state cannot exclude religious schools from a private school choice program once it creates such a program.

Ruled that Philadelphia could not refuse to contract with a Catholic social services agency that would not certify same-sex couples as foster parents, because the city's non-discrimination policy was not "generally applicable."
*Mahanoy Area School Dist. v. B.L.*, 594 U.S. \_\_\_
Ruled that a school violated a student's First Amendment rights by punishing her for off-campus speech (a profane Snapchat post).
Protected the right of a high school football coach to engage in personal prayer on the field after games.
Ruled that a state that provides tuition assistance for private schools cannot exclude schools that provide religious instruction.
